To secretly feel pleased about the death or decline of others when we stand to benefit from it, or with their downfall when we might prosper because of it, is a sin that easily tempts us, yet isn't considered as bad as it truly is. But it comes from a selfish, covetous principle and from that love of the world as our happiness, which God’s love expressly forbids. He often ruins the plans of those who would raise themselves on the ruin of others. The common sayings in the business world are directly opposed to God's law. But he will show his opposition to money-loving, selfish traders whose hearts, like those of Tyre, are hardened by the love of riches. People have little reason to boast about things that stir up the envy and greed of others, and which constantly change hands; and in obtaining, keeping, and spending them, people provoke God whose anger turns joyful cities into ruinous heaps.

See how high, how great Tyre had been. See how low Tyre is now. The fall of others should wake us from security. Every discovery of the fulfillment of a Scripture prophecy is like a miracle to confirm our faith. All that is earthly is vanity and distress. Those who currently have the most established prosperity will soon be out of sight and forgotten.
